title: Firewood Holder Console Table
summary: A long, low console with a plywood-lined firewood tray built into a 2x4 frame, angled front legs, and a full plywood back panel — genuine firewood storage furniture, not a shelf repurposed for logs.
description:
Two identical 2x4 frames form the top and bottom of this piece, each lined with a sheet of 3/8" plywood to keep bark and debris contained rather than resting logs directly against bare 2x4 rails. Build both frames with 3" screws, two per joint, then attach the plywood tray liner to the bottom frame with 3/4" brad nails and glue.

**The front legs are a paired A-frame style, while the back gets a single flat leg at each end** — attach the front leg pairs to the front of the trays first, keeping all outside edges flush, then add the single back legs. The full plywood back panel goes on next, closing off the back of the piece entirely — this is what turns an open firewood cradle into an actual enclosed console.

The top is a 1x12 board with 1x8 edge trim, overhanging the frame below. **Additional supports or hidden storage can be built into the top** if you want a lift-top compartment, but the base build is a straightforward open firewood tray console without one.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four frame long rails at 66-1/2" and four frame short rails at 17" from the 2x4. Cut one bottom tray liner at 69-1/2" from the 3/8" plywood, full sheet. Cut four front legs at 31-3/4" and two back legs at 31-3/4" from the 1x4. Cut one back panel at 71" from the 3/8" plywood, full sheet. Cut one top edge trim at 71" from the 1x8. Cut one top board at 71" from the 1x12. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft 2x4, two 8 ft 1x4, one 6 ft 1x8 and one 6 ft 1x12. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the frames
steps.body: Each frame is a rectangle of two 66-1/2" long rails and two 17" short rails, and there are two identical frames. The 17" short rails run the full depth at each end with the long rails fitting between them, which makes each frame 69-1/2" long overall and 17" deep, with a clear opening of 66-1/2" x 10" inside — that 69-1/2" is exactly the length of the plywood tray liner, which is how the assembly reads. Drive two 3" self-tapping screws through the face of each short rail into the end grain of the long rail, working on a flat floor so nothing builds in a twist. Measure both diagonals of each frame and adjust until they match before the last screws go tight, then set the two frames side by side and confirm they are the same size as each other.

steps.title: Line the bottom tray
steps.body: The tray liner is 69-1/2" x 16-1/2", and 69-1/2" is the frame's full outside length — so it lies flat ON TOP of the bottom frame rather than dropping inside it, since the inside opening is only 66-1/2" x 10". Run a bead of glue along every rail, lay the liner down, and fix it with 3/4" brad nails into all four rails, keeping the nails back from the plywood edges so they don't blow out through the 3/8" edge. The liner is 16-1/2" against the frame's 17" depth, so you have 1/2" to place: set it flush at the back where the panel will close it off and let the 1/2" show at the front. The whole point of the liner is that bark and debris land on plywood rather than dropping between the rails, so seal the joint at the perimeter with glue as you go.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: All six legs are 31-3/4" 1x4. The four front legs go on in pairs — two at each end on the FRONT face of the two frames — and the two back legs go one at each end on the BACK face, which is what makes the front read as a paired A-frame and the back a single flat leg. Because the legs land on the outside faces rather than between the rails, the finished depth is 3/4" + 17" + 3/4" = 18-1/2", exactly the combined width of the 11-1/4" top board and the 7-1/4" edge trim that go on later. Clamp the top frame flush with the leg tops and the bottom frame flush with the leg bottoms, keep every outside edge flush, and check the piece stands square before fastening with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails driven through the 3/4" leg into the 2x4 rails. Set that way the clear firewood opening between the two frames works out at 24-3/4"; the source does not state the frame heights, so if you want a different tray opening, decide it now — everything above depends on it.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: The back panel is 31-1/2" x 71" of 3/8" plywood and it goes on the OUTSIDE of the two back legs, closing the back of the piece completely — this is the part that turns an open firewood cradle into a console. At 71" it is 3/4" longer than the 69-1/2" frames at each end, and it sets the same 3/4" overhang the 71" top will have, so align it by its ends rather than by the legs. It is 31-1/2" tall against 31-3/4" legs; line the panel up with the leg tops and let the 1/4" difference fall at the floor where it will not show. Glue and 3/4" brads into both back legs and into the back rail of each frame, working from the middle outward so the panel doesn't bow.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: The top is two boards laid side by side: the 11-1/4" wide 1x12 top board and the 7-1/4" wide 1x8 edge trim, both 71" long, making an 18-1/2" wide top. Glue the two long edges together and clamp them flat, then nail the assembly down into the top frame rails with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Lengthwise the 71" top overhangs the 69-1/2" frame by 3/4" at each end, flush with the back panel; front to back the 18-1/2" top exactly matches the 18-1/2" leg-to-leg depth, so it finishes flush at the front and, because the 3/8" back panel sits outside the back legs, stops 3/8" shy at the back — set it flush at the front and let that 3/8" land behind. Additional supports or a hinged lift-top compartment can be built into the top if you want hidden storage, but the base build is the plain fixed top described here.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill every brad hole, then sand the whole piece — start coarse on the 2x4 frames, which come rough from the mill, and finish fine on the top where hands land. Ease the top's edges and both end corners with the sander so nobody catches a shin on them, and knock the sharp arris off the tray liner's edges too. Apply paint or stain to every face including the inside of the tray, since that surface takes the abuse from bark and split ends. Let the finish cure fully before you load any wood into it.

images.alt: Firewood holder console table with a plywood-lined tray
